epigraph.text: Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights.
epigraph.quote: The topology of the domain determines which functions have antiderivatives.

content.text:
Cauchy's theorem depends critically on the domain having no "holes." 
We make this precise with the concept of simple connectivity.

content.title: Simply Connected Domain
content.content:
A domain $D$ is **simply connected** 
if every simple closed curve in $D$ can be continuously shrunk to a 
point while remaining in $D$. Equivalently, $D$ has no "holes."

content.title: Simply connected domains
content.problem:
- The entire plane $\C$ is simply connected.

- Any open disk is simply connected.

- A half-plane (e.g., $\re(z) > 0$) is simply connected.

- The punctured plane $\C \setminus \{0\}$ is *not* simply 
 connected—a loop around the origin cannot shrink to a point.

- An annulus $\{z : 1 < |z| < 2\}$ is *not* simply connected.

content.text: Cauchy's Theorem for Simply Connected Domains

content.title: Cauchy's Theorem (Simply Connected)
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on a simply connected domain $D$. Then for every 
closed contour $\gamma$ in $D$:
\[
 \oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 0.
\]
Moreover, $f$ has an antiderivative on $D$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This is the most useful form of Cauchy's theorem. Simple connectivity 
ensures that any closed contour can be triangulated within $D$.

content.title: Deformation Principle
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on a domain $D$. If $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are 
contours in $D$ with the same endpoints, and $\gamma_1$ can be 
continuously deformed to $\gamma_2$ within $D$, then
\[
 \int_{\gamma_1} f(z)\,dz = \int_{\gamma_2} f(z)\,dz.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The closed contour $\gamma_1 - \gamma_2$ (traverse $\gamma_1$ then 
$\gamma_2$ reversed) bounds a region in $D$. By Cauchy's theorem,
\[
 \oint_{\gamma_1 - \gamma_2} f = 0 \implies 
 \int_{\gamma_1} f = \int_{\gamma_2} f.
\]

content.text: For domains with holes, we have a modified version:

content.title: Cauchy's Theorem (Multiply Connected)
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on a domain $D$, and let $\gamma$ be a contour that 
can be expressed as an outer boundary $\gamma_0$ minus inner boundaries 
$\gamma_1, \ldots, \gamma_n$ (all traversed counterclockwise). Then
\[
 \oint_{\gamma_0} f\,dz = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \oint_{\gamma_k} f\,dz.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This allows us to compute integrals over complicated contours by 
replacing them with simpler ones encircling the same singularities.

marginNotes.text:
Jesus said to him, "I am the way, and the 
truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me."

marginNotes.author: Henri Poincaré
marginNotes.text:
Geometry is the art of reasoning correctly 
from incorrectly drawn figures.
